msgs.text: Is this a correct mathematical proof of the presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 2-adic numbers as a profinite group?

No. **The presentation may be plausible, but the manuscript is not a complete proof as written.** The decisive gap is in §9.3.

From \(0\to T_0\to M\to V\to0\), the deformation complexes give a connecting map
\[
H^1_\rho(M)\longrightarrow H^1_\rho(V)\longrightarrow H^2_\rho(T_0).
\]
The paper never proves that the last map vanishes. In fact, its own Proposition 5.2 says
\[
H^2_\rho(T_0)^\vee\simeq (T_0^\vee)^C,
\]
which is generally nonzero because \(T_0\) has trivial composition factors. Consequently, the \(V\)-coordinate \(c\) arising from \(M\)-lifts may range over only a proper affine subspace of \(H^1_\rho(V)\). The zero-counts for the quadratic form \(Q\) on the **whole** space, proved in Propositions 6.3 and 6.6, then do not justify formula (31).

The manuscript replaces this missing argument with the sentences “filter … through its scalar radical,” “if the filtration-one edge … is nonzero,” and “let \(\mu\) be the common multiplicity.” That edge is never defined, and no exact-sequence argument establishes either the asserted dichotomy or the uniform multiplicity \(\mu\). Since formula (31) drives the central \(R\)-stage and hence the global induction, this is a fatal gap, not a minor omitted calculation. 

Other substantial omissions include the unshown Heisenberg/Fox computation underlying formulas (12)–(13), the new Shapiro–quadratic calculation in Lemma 6.5, and compatibility of the chosen maximal pro-\(2\) generators with the fixed tame quotient. The early construction and the tame/maximal-pro-\(2\) checks look broadly plausible, but they do not establish the full absolute Galois group.

This would also constitute a solution to a problem still listed as unsolved in June 2026, so it requires particularly careful independent verification.
Ok, please let us know how to address the gaps that you pointed out. Think through this rivetingly, novelly, and describe the manuscript edits we should make

## The central repair: do not force surjectivity—Fourier-transform the missing directions

The paragraph surrounding (31) should be replaced, not patched. From
\[
0\longrightarrow T_0\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow V\longrightarrow0
\]
and \(H^2_\rho(M)=0\), the long exact sequence gives
\[
H^1_\rho(M)\longrightarrow H^1_\rho(V)
 \xrightarrow{\partial_\rho}H^2_\rho(T_0)\longrightarrow0.
\]
Thus the \(V\)-coordinates of \(M\)-lifts form an affine fiber of \(\partial_\rho\), generally a proper affine subspace. When \(T_0\neq0\), one ordinarily has
\[
H^2_\rho(T_0)^\vee\simeq (T_0^\vee)^C\neq0,
\]
so trying to prove that all of \(H^1_\rho(V)\) occurs is the wrong strategy. The manuscript should explicitly acknowledge this immediately before replacing the present argument on pages 15–16. 

The clean repair is an **affine quadratic Fourier transform**.

### 1. Insert an affine quadratic Fourier lemma in §8

Add the following after Lemma 8.2.

> **Lemma 8.3 (Constrained quadratic Gauss transform).**  
> Let \(H,E\) be finite \(\mathbf F_2\)-vector spaces, let
> \[
> L:H\twoheadrightarrow E,
> \]
> and let \(Q:H\to\mathbf F_2\) be nonsingular, with polar form \(B_Q\). For \(\kappa\in E\) and \(\epsilon\in\mathbf F_2\), put
> \[
> N(\kappa,\epsilon)
> =\#\{x\in H:Lx=\kappa,\ Q(x)=\epsilon\}.
> \]
> For each \(\chi\in E^\vee\), let \(a_\chi\in H\) be uniquely determined by
> \[
> B_Q(a_\chi,x)=\chi(Lx).
> \]
> Then
> \[
> N(\kappa,\epsilon)
> =
> \frac{1}{2|E|}
> \left(
> |H|+
> G(Q)\sum_{\chi\in E^\vee}
> (-1)^{\chi(\kappa)+\epsilon+Q(a_\chi)}
> \right),
> \tag{24'}
> \]
> where
> \[
> G(Q)=\sum_{x\in H}(-1)^{Q(x)}
> =\#Q^{-1}(0)-\#Q^{-1}(1).
> \]

The proof is only a few lines: expand both conditions using additive characters, use surjectivity of \(L\), and complete the square:
\[
Q(x)+B_Q(a_\chi,x)=Q(x+a_\chi)+Q(a_\chi).
\]

This lemma captures precisely what (31) overlooks. For a quadratic form on an affine hyperplane, its total Arf invariant is not enough; one must also know the quadratic values at the vectors dual to the defining linear equations.

---

## 2. Define the previously unnamed filtration-one edge

Fix \(\lambda\neq0\), write \(T=T_0\), and let
\[
\alpha_\lambda\in H^2(B,\mathbf F_2)
\]
be the class of \(B_\lambda\to B\). Proposition 7.4 says that its restriction to \(M\) is the quadratic map \(q_\lambda\), whose radical is exactly \(T\).

Add a lemma giving a concrete definition.

> **Lemma 9.x (Radical edge and descent).**  
> The class \(\alpha_\lambda\) determines a canonical edge
> \[
> \varepsilon_\lambda\in H^1(B/T,T^\vee).
> \]
> Its restriction to \(V=M/T\) is zero, so it is inflated from a unique class
> \[
> \bar\varepsilon_\lambda\in H^1(C,T^\vee).
> \]
> If \(\bar\varepsilon_\lambda=0\), then \(\alpha_\lambda\) is inflated from a class in
> \[
> H^2(B/T,\mathbf F_2).
> \]

A useful group-level definition is this. Since \(q_\lambda|_T=0\) and \(b_\lambda(T,M)=0\), the preimage of \(T\) in \(B_\lambda\) is a split elementary abelian extension. Choose a complement \(s(T)\). For \(\bar b\in B/T\), define
\[
\widetilde b\,s(t)\,\widetilde b^{-1}
 =
s({}^{b}t)\,z^{\varepsilon_\lambda(\bar b)(t)},
\]
where \(z\) generates the central kernel. Changing the complement changes the cocycle by a coboundary. Its restriction to \(V\) vanishes because \(b_\lambda(T,M)=0\).

This gives a rigorous replacement for the phrase “the filtration-one edge along \(T_0\).”

If \(\bar\varepsilon_\lambda\neq0\), its pullback through every epimorphism \(\rho:\Gamma\twoheadrightarrow C\) remains nonzero. Perfect duality then makes the obstruction a nonzero linear functional on the \(T\)-lift directions. Consequently
\[
Z_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B/C)
=
2^{2\dim M-1}|X_\Gamma(C)|.
\tag{30a}
\]
This proves the “exactly half” branch without any unspecified multiplicity.

---

## 3. In the zero-edge branch, retain the affine lifting condition

Suppose \(\bar\varepsilon_\lambda=0\). Then the cover descends to \(B/T\). Lemma 6.8 still implies
\[
B/T\simeq V\rtimes C,
\]
and Lemma 6.9 can still remove the mixed determinant component. After transporting the \(T\)-extension along that shear, the descended cover class has the form
\[
q+\operatorname{inf}_C^{V\rtimes C}\delta,
\qquad \delta\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2).
\tag{30b}
\]

Now define the data controlling which \(V\)-cocycles actually lift to \(B\).

Let
\[
\kappa\in H^2(C,T)
\]
be the class obtained by pulling \(B\to V\rtimes C\) back along the zero section \(C\to V\rtimes C\). Let
\[
\partial_\rho:H^1_\rho(V)\longrightarrow H^2_\rho(T)
\]
be the connecting map associated with \(0\to T\to M\to V\to0\).

Add:

> **Lemma 9.y (Affine \(T\)-lifting equation).**  
> A \(V\)-cohomology class \(c\in H^1_\rho(V)\) lifts to an \(M\)-lift if and only if
> \[
> \partial_\rho(c)=\rho^*\kappa.
> \tag{30c}
> \]
> For every such \(c\), the number of raw lifts over it is
> \[
> |B^1_\rho(V)|\,|Z^1_\rho(T)|.
> \]

This should be proved from an explicit cocycle for the extension \(B\to V\rtimes C\). Its pure \(V\times V\) component is zero because \(M\) is elementary abelian; its mixed component is exactly the module extension \(0\to T\to M\to V\to0\).

This lemma eliminates the unjustified assumption that the \(c\)-variable ranges freely.

---

## 4. Identify the Fourier phases as ordinary central covers of \(C\)

Put
\[
D=(T^\vee)^C,\qquad r=\dim D,\qquad d=\dim V.
\]
By duality,
\[
D\simeq H^2_\rho(T)^\vee.
\]

For \(\chi\in D\), take the boundary in the dual module sequence
\[
0\longrightarrow V^\vee\longrightarrow M^\vee
\longrightarrow T^\vee\longrightarrow0:
\]
\[
\partial_C^\vee(\chi)\in H^1(C,V^\vee).
\]
Let \(b_q^\flat:V\to V^\vee\) be the polar isomorphism and define
\[
a_\chi=(b_q^\flat)^{-1}\partial_C^\vee(\chi)
\in H^1(C,V).
\]

The manuscript needs the following corollary of deformation duality.

> **Corollary 5.3 (Adjoint connecting homomorphisms).**  
> For every \(c\in H^1_\rho(V)\) and \(\chi\in D\),
> \[
> \langle\partial_\rho c,\chi\rangle
> =
> B_{Q,\rho}\bigl(c,\rho^*a_\chi\bigr).
> \tag{11'}
> \]

On the local side this is the usual functoriality of Tate duality. On the presentation side it follows from the naturality of the chain map \(D_{\rho,A}\); that is why the Fox–Heisenberg calculation must establish chain-level naturality, not merely equality of dimensions.

Next define
\[
\mathfrak P_q(a_\chi)\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2)
\]
to be the determinant connecting class obtained by pulling
\[
1\longrightarrow\mathbf F_2
\longrightarrow\widetilde V_q\rtimes C
\longrightarrow V\rtimes C
\longrightarrow1
\]
back along the complement
\[
c\longmapsto (a_\chi(c),c).
\]
Set
\[
\Delta_\chi
=
\chi_*(\kappa)+\mathfrak P_q(a_\chi)
\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2).
\tag{30d}
\]

Naturality gives
\[
Q_\rho(\rho^*a_\chi)
+\langle\rho^*\kappa,\chi\rangle
=
\operatorname{inv}_\Gamma\!\bigl(\rho^*\Delta_\chi\bigr).
\tag{30e}
\]

---

## 5. Replace formula (31) by the corrected formula

Let
\[
G(Q)=N_0(Q)-N_1(Q).
\]
Propositions 6.3 and 6.6 prove that \(G(Q)\) is identical on the candidate and local sides.

For a fixed lower map \(\rho\), Lemma 8.3 gives
\[
\#\left\{
c:
\partial_\rho c=\rho^*\kappa,\;
Q(c)+\rho^*\delta=0
\right\}
=
\frac{1}{2^{r+1}}
\left(
2^d+
G(Q)\sum_{\chi\in D}
(-1)^{\operatorname{inv}_\Gamma
(\rho^*(\delta+\Delta_\chi))}
\right).
\tag{31a}
\]

The previously unnamed multiplicity is now explicit:
\[
\mu
=
|B^1_\rho(V)|\,|Z^1_\rho(T)|
=
2^d\,2^{2\dim T+r}.
\tag{31b}
\]
The equality of this number on the two sides follows directly from Proposition 5.2 and the local Euler–Poincaré formula.

Define
\[
S_\Gamma(\zeta)
=
\sum_{\rho\in X_\Gamma(C)}
(-1)^{\operatorname{inv}_\Gamma(\rho^*\zeta)}.
\]
Then the replacement for (31) is
\[
\boxed{
Z_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B/C)
=
\frac{\mu}{2^{r+1}}
\left(
2^d|X_\Gamma(C)|
+
G(Q)\sum_{\chi\in D}
S_\Gamma(\delta+\Delta_\chi)
\right).
}
\tag{31'}
\]

For any \(\zeta\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2)\),
\[
S_\Gamma(\zeta)
=
2n_{\Gamma,0}(\zeta)-|X_\Gamma(C)|,
\]
where \(n_{\Gamma,0}(\zeta)\) counts lower maps for which the central cover represented by \(\zeta\) is unobstructed. Apply the existing central-cover transform (32) separately to each class
\[
\zeta=\delta+\Delta_\chi.
\]
The same descent estimate applies:
\[
2|L_C|
=
\frac{2|L_Y|}{|K|}
<|L_Y|.
\]
Thus every \(S_\Gamma(\delta+\Delta_\chi)\) agrees by induction.

This repairs the central stage without requiring any identification between the candidate and local \(H^1(V)\)-spaces. It uses only:

\[
\text{matching Gauss sums}
+\text{ functorial duality}
+\text{ smaller central-cover counts}.
\]

The old formula (31) is precisely the special case \(T_0=0\), when \(D=0\). That explains why it looks correct but fails in the presence of the scalar radical.

---

# Supporting manuscript repairs

## A. Expand the Fox–Heisenberg calculation in §5

The sentences preceding (12)–(13) should become a proposition with an auditable computation. State the evaluated Jacobian explicitly:
\[
J_{\rho,V}=
\begin{pmatrix}
1+T & 1+S(1+T) & 0 & 0\\
0 & P & 0 & P+S^{-1}
\end{pmatrix}.
\tag{12'}
\]
Then show that representatives of \(H^1_\rho(V)\) can be chosen with only the \(x_0\)-coordinate nonzero, and calculate the mixed Hessian:
\[
\mathcal B_\rho(c,c')
=
\begin{cases}
B(c,c'),&T=1,\\[2mm]
B\bigl(c,(1+U+U^{-1})c'\bigr),&V^T=0.
\end{cases}
\tag{13'}
\]

The appendix should include:

1. Fox rules for powers, inverses, conjugates, and commutators.
2. The first derivatives of both relators.
3. The nonzero second-derivative terms.
4. Verification that the relation trace gives a chain map.
5. The scalar specialization yielding table (7).

The existing machine-readable exponent is useful as a checksum, but it does not replace these universal identities.

## B. Rewrite Lemma 6.5 as three smaller lemmas

The local argument needs these details made explicit:

1. **Filtration multiplicities.** Write
   \[
   \operatorname{gr}_j M_K\simeq k_K(\theta^j)
   \]
   for odd \(j<2e\), and explain that Hilbert duality pairs depths \(j\) and \(2e-j\). The middle depth \(j=e\) has trivial inertia, so it contains no copy of the ramified \(V\). Therefore exactly half of the \(V\)-isotypic contribution lies above depth \(e\).

2. **Regular-module Shapiro formula.** Write the pulled-back quadratic polynomial as
   \[
   \sum c_{r,s,g}\sum_hX_{r,h}X_{s,hg}
   +
   \sum d_r\sum_hX_{r,h}^2
   \]
   and prove directly that the two orbit types map respectively to
   \[
   \operatorname{cor}_{K/\mathbf Q_2}
   (\alpha_r\smile g\alpha_s),
   \qquad
   \operatorname{cor}_{K/\mathbf Q_2}
   (\alpha_r\smile\alpha_r).
   \]

3. **Unit-filtration vanishing.** Use
   \[
   U_{e+1}\subseteq U_{e+1}^{\perp}=U_e,
   \qquad -1\in U_e,
   \]
   and explicitly invoke
   \[
   \alpha\smile\alpha=\alpha\smile(-1).
   \]

That turns the current sketch into a reproducible cochain calculation.

## C. Add a compatible local-marking lemma after Proposition 3.3

The manuscript currently chooses the tame and maximal pro-\(2\) markings separately. Add a lemma proving that they can be chosen simultaneously.

The relevant map should be displayed:
\[
G_{\mathbf Q_2}
\twoheadrightarrow
T_{\mathrm{tame}}
\times_{\mathbf Z_2}
\Pi.
\]
The common quotient is the unramified pro-\(2\) direction. Choose the Demushkin basis compatibly with it; at the mod-\(2\) Kummer level, the square classes
\[
[5],\ [-1],\ [2]
\]
give the desired Hilbert matrix: the unramified line is \([5]\), while the nonzero pairings are the \([5]\)-\([2]\) pairing and the self-pairing of \([-1]\). Then quote the relative Demushkin normal-form theorem yielding
\[
x_0^2x_1^4[x_1,\sigma]=1,
\]
which is the same as
\[
x_0^2x_1^3x_1^\sigma=1
\]
under the manuscript’s commutator convention.

Section 4 should then say that all tame frames and scalar decorations use this fixed compatible marking. Incompatible tame/decorative data give empty counting sets on both sides.

## D. Editorial changes

Delete the current text from “Filter the elementary module \(M\) first through its scalar radical” through old formula (31). Replace it with the edge lemma, affine lifting lemma, phase construction, and formula (31′).

Also:

- Add the adjoint-boundary corollary after Proposition 5.2.
- Generalize (32) by replacing \(\delta\) with every \(\delta+\Delta_\chi\).
- Add \(B/T_0\) and the auxiliary phase covers \(C_{\delta+\Delta_\chi}\) to the quotient ledger.
- Move the machine serialization behind the human-verifiable Fox–Heisenberg appendix.
- Give exact theorem or proposition numbers for every imported Demushkin, filtration, and Tate-duality result.

This is a substantial but focused repair. It preserves the global induction and the determinant computations, while replacing the one invalid whole-space count with a constrained Gauss-sum calculation that correctly remembers the scalar radical.
